pi_items.source: export_result.sh macro script (line 18) and Fiji sample images documentation; actual image name is 'Fluorescent Cells (400K)' not just 'Fluorescent Cells'
export_result.sh confirms split channels yields 'Fluorescent Cells (400K) (red)' window (line 22); web search confirms F-Actin staining with Texas Red X-Phalloidin represents red channel

Specified in task.json and used in export_result.sh macro (lines 34-37); represents Low Positive (1+) intensity tier in H-Score formula; divides 8-bit image range appropriately; formula matches standard H-Score methodology from immunohistochemistry literature
Specified in task.json and used in export_result.sh macro (lines 39-42); represents Medium Positive (2+) intensity tier in H-Score formula; part of standard H-Score methodology
Specified in task.json and used in export_result.sh macro (lines 45-48); represents High Positive (3+) intensity tier in H-Score formula; covers maximum intensity range in 8-bit images
export_result.sh script (line 82) explicitly uses this path for result file; setup_task.sh clears this file at startup (line 16)

Task description (line 6) and export_result.sh macro (line 58) both implement this formula; matches standard immunohistochemistry H-Score methodology from peer-reviewed literature (PMC11141889, PMC10396076)

privileged_info_summary: The task uses the real Fluorescent Cells (400K) sample image from ImageJ, which contains bovine pulmonary artery endothelial cells with three fluorescent channels: Blue nucleus (DAPI), Green Tubulin (Bodipy FL), and Red F-Actin (Texas Red X-Phalloidin). The red channel intensity range (0-255) is divided into four tiers: background (<20), Low (20-99), Medium (100-179), High (180-255). The H-Score formula is correctly specified as (1×%Low) + (2×%Medium) + (3×%High), matching standard immunohistochemistry methodology. Results are saved to /home/ga/ImageJ_Data/results/h_score_report.csv. The metadata sample_name is incomplete (claims 'Fluorescent Cells' instead of full name 'Fluorescent Cells (400K)'), though the task description and scripts correctly reference the full image name.
